title: Tale of Gajendra, the elephant and Grah, the crocodile

analysis.core_story_summary: King Parikshit asked, "Munivar, God had salve Gajendra, in His Hari incarnation. I wish to hear this tale. " Shukdev says, " O king, in Tamas Manvantara, God had appeared as Hari and saved Gajendra from the jaws of Grah the crocodile.

audience_notes.children_8_12: Use simple language, explain divine names as characters in a sacred legend.
audience_notes.teens_13_17: Connect events to decision-making, identity, and consequences.
audience_notes.young_adults_18_30: Highlight ethics, leadership, and social relevance.
audience_notes.devotional_traditional: Preserve sacred framing and theological terms respectfully.
audience_notes.global_general: Keep cultural context brief and avoid insider assumptions.

sensitivity_notes: Contains conflict imagery that may need age-appropriate framing.
Includes intense mythic elements (curses, demons, destruction).

redesigned_story_by_demographic.children_8_12: **Tale of Gajendra, the elephant and Grah, the crocodile — Kids (8-12):** King Parikshit asked, "Munivar, God had salve Gajendra, in His Hari incarnation. I wish to hear this tale. " Shukdev says, " O king, in Tamas Manvantara, God had appeared as Hari and saved Gajendra from the jaws of Grah the crocodile. This story teaches devotion and disciplined practice, dedication and sacrifice, the cost of conflict. Think of it like a legend that helps us choose kindness, courage, and respect.
